opp.ais_why:
• On the 2/26/2026 demo, evaluator Michael Henderson praised Zycus's ability to suggest autonomous sourcing beyond the vendor master and asked that conversational tactical sourcing be enabled for mid-size spends (the example was 50 chairs). Mary Stapleton called catalog-in-chat a big step forward for end users. Both are buyer-driven autonomous sourcing signals.
• Collins Farr asked deep orchestration questions, including whether Cornell can upload purchase policies as source documents the AI references when responding to user requests. Michael Henderson asked whether the AI text box could sit in front of SSO and pop up there. These are not check-the-box AI questions; they are deployment-level thinking.
• The need spans Merlin Intake, federated AI search, AI-driven RFQ and negotiation via Anna, AI price benchmarking, and AP Smartdesk. A stakeholder demo is set for mid-March and the path to NDA and budget is defined. The 64-minute demo shows real AI hunger, but until budget is confirmed, confidence sits at Medium.

metrics.narrative: Cornell has a hard, quantified target: Henderson said they are 'formulating around a pretty significant savings goal... 60,000,000 plus on a spend base of a little over 1,100,000,000' (intro call, 6 Feb 2026), restated and confirmed on the 24 Feb discovery call. The driver is austerity: 'a piece of why we're interested in some of the AI native software platforms is how can we accelerate outcomes without necessarily having to burden the p and l with more staff' (Henderson, 6 Feb). Urgency is explicit, 'we can't wait until 2028 to get started, it needs to start in the next 6 months.' The open metrics work is converting that $60M target into a per-phase savings model tied to Zycus, which is a ranked move.

champion.narrative: Michael Henderson (CPO, started week-3 at first call) is a genuine, active champion: he has personally driven every call, articulated the savings mandate, pushed to 'open the aperture' to wider stakeholders, and SF records him as the Champion/Business Buyer ('Michael Henderson, CPO, and Cooper Smith, Director of Contract Management', X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c). He is supported by Cooper Smith (Director of Contract Management), who engages on detailed integration and sourcing questions. What is still missing is champion reach UP: Henderson is new and openly unsure of his internal partners ('I don't know our partners as well as I should yet', 24 Feb), so his access to the economic buyer and IT leadership is unproven. Strength is strong at the procurement level, developing on the path to power.

competition.narrative: This is a two-horse race that has narrowed to Zycus vs Zip: 'Prospect confirmed that it is between us and Zip' (SF Next_Step_History__c, 4 Mar and 9 Mar 2026), and Henderson himself said 'I've been between you and Zip, I've been waiting to see who kind of emerges at the top of the stack' (6 Feb). Zip is the live, strongest current threat, strong on intake but, per Zycus, weaker on completeness of vision. Coupa was evaluated but the CPO believes 'Coupa's value prop has faded' (SF Next_Step_History__c, 26 Feb) — now low/declining. Fairmarkit and Globality are sourcing-only point tools the buyer can run un-integrated via CSV. Jagger (Ithaca) and SAP Ariba (Weill Cornell Medicine) are the incumbents being displaced. Our wedge is platform completeness plus Merlin Intake in Teams/Slack, named in SF as 'a massive differentiator here for us... what they are most interested in' (Zycus_Differentiation_Why_Zycus__c).

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific and confirmed by the buyer. Workday will not meet Cornell's P2P requirements ('Implementing Workday and it will not check their boxes in terms of P2P requirements', SF Customer_Business_Problem__c). Henderson detailed gaps: Workday's draft sourcing module has 'no baked-in autonomy', central IT finds catalogs 'hard to maintain and build in Workday', and Workday has 'no shopper role, just buyer' which breaks how faculty and facilities staff actually purchase (24/26 Feb). Operationally, a small sourcing team must hit a $60M savings target on $1.1B spend without adding headcount, and they over-rely on sole-source procurements they want AI to displace (24 Feb). Pain owner is procurement leadership (Henderson, Smith), with end-user (faculty/staff) self-service as the felt symptom.

sources: SF Next_Step_History__c (17 Mar-14 Apr 2026, NDA executed, order form sent)
paper_process.narrative: Paper process has started but stalled in visibility. An NDA was executed and a sample order form was sent to Cornell 'to get ahead of potential legal roadblocks' (SF Next_Step_History__c, entries 17 Mar through 14 Apr 2026), and the pricing-call requirements list explicitly includes a sample MSA. The 23 Jun Next Step invited the 'director of contracts' to Horizon, signalling the contracting owner is identified but not yet actively redlining. Given Cornell's size (multi-entity: Ithaca, Weill Cornell Medicine NYC and Qatar) and the $450k 3-year scope, InfoSec review and ERP integration scoping (Workday plus legacy SAP Ariba and Jagger) are mandatory gates not yet opened, adding 4-6 weeks minimum. There is no incumbent-contract expiration forcing function (SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c blank).

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is engaged. Henderson (CPO, week-3 hire) was candid: 'It'd be silly of me or misleading to say that Cooper and I are the sole business makers, we're probably more gonna influence' and 'we haven't figured out yet how we make a decision' (24 Feb 2026). The only C-level signal is Zycus-side: the Zycus founder/CEO (a Cornell graduate) 'expressed a keen interest in being executive sponsor' (24 Feb). Cornell's budget owner (likely a CFO or Provost under broad austerity) is unnamed across all calls, tasks, contact roles, and the $450k 3-year deal. The 23 Jun Next Step invited the CPO and director of contracts to Horizon but no budget-authority conversation is logged. Mapping and meeting Cornell's economic buyer is the single highest-leverage move.

sources: SF Next_Step_History__c (9 Mar 2026 — Huron Consulting, exec alignment)
decision_process.narrative: The process is forming and partly visible. The call sequence shows a deliberate enterprise motion: intro 6 Feb, discovery 24 Feb, demo 26 Feb (broader stakeholders incl. Mary Stapleton and Collins Farr joined), preliminary pricing 4 Mar with the CPO and Director of Contract Management. Henderson plans to 'craft a narrative' for IT partners and 'open the aperture' to the wider procurement org before a mid-March business-stakeholder review (24/26 Feb). A named SI is in the loop: 'Working through Huron Consulting' (SF Next_Step_History__c, 9 Mar). What is still undefined is the formal approval chain and who signs off the $450k, which Henderson openly admitted is not yet decided. Salesforce What_is_the_decision_process__c is blank, but the calls carry the live picture.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are well-documented from the discovery and demo. Cornell is evaluating which Source-to-Pay pieces best augment its Workday rollout, leading with eProcurement intake/orchestration, sourcing (strategic and autonomous), and spend analysis (24 Feb 2026). Specific must-haves surfaced: Merlin intake guided buying that works while requisitioning stays in Workday without end users logging into Zycus; federated catalog search across ~140 catalogs (to be rationalized to 12-15) with part-number cross-reference and stock; handling Workday's goods/services split-table structure; both flavors of autonomous sourcing (bid collection and AI negotiation with human-in-the-loop); and an iSave CFO dashboard separating savings pipeline, realized savings, and cost avoided 'as three different measures' (Henderson, 24 Feb). References to current Workday customers were named as important but 'not a dealbreaker' (SF Why_not_Zycus__c).

Co-author the mutual close plan with Henderson and the director of contracts to reset the October date to a credible Q1 2027 milestone. The current close date is a placeholder (no movement since create, no forcing function). Map every remaining gate (pricing finalization, ROI workshop, EB/CFO proposal, InfoSec review, ERP integration deep-dive, MSA/SOW contracting), name the owner and due date for each, and tie the new close date to a real buyer milestone (e.g. fiscal Q1 2027 budget cycle, or Phase 1 go-live target). This honest re-plan strengthens trust and forecast defensibility.
Zip competitive threat active, differentiation wedge not yet activated

Activate Zycus CEO (Cornell alum) executive-sponsor lever to unlock Henderson's access to CFO/Provost. The CEO offered to serve as exec sponsor on 24 Feb ('you've got him in your pocket when you need him'). Use this to create exec-to-exec pull that differentiates from Zip and opens the door to the EB — the single biggest close risk. Schedule a CEO-to-Henderson alignment call within 14 days, then position a CEO-to-CFO/Provost intro as the natural next step.

Make Merlin autonomous negotiation + Workday integration the Phase 1 hero at the mid-March business-stakeholder review. Cornell's $60M savings goal under austerity (no hiring) and the need to empower distributed faculty/staff self-service sourcing are Zycus's strongest wedge vs Zip (intake-only). Prepare a 2026–2028 intercept roadmap: Phase 1 = catalog rationalization + autonomous sourcing go-live in 2026 (capture quick wins, prove ROI), Phase 2 = full Workday integration post-Jul 2028 go-live. Make this the narrative Henderson pitches to IT and the EB.
best_practice_check.summary: Three deal-aware win plays for a healthy Zycus-vs-Zip race that is well-positioned functionally but exposed on power and timing.

competitive_position.summary: Ranked by current threat: (1) Zip is the live primary threat, the buyer confirmed 'it is between us and Zip' (4/9 Mar 2026) and Henderson named Zip as the rival he was watching; strong on intake, per Zycus weaker on completeness of vision. (2) Jagger (Ithaca) and SAP Ariba (Weill Cornell Medicine) are the incumbents being displaced. (3) Coupa was evaluated but the CPO believes its value prop has faded (26 Feb 2026) — now low. (4) Fairmarkit and Globality are sourcing-only point tools, low integrated threat. Our wedge across all of them is one platform (intake + sourcing + spend analytics + Workday integration) with Merlin Intake in Teams/Slack as the hero.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional fit on the buyer's top criteria (Merlin Intake over Workday, federated catalog search, autonomous sourcing, spend analytics with CFO dashboards). The main open fit question is the Workday goods/services split-table integration, which Zycus said it solves via cloud middleware but must prove with an IT-partner session.

hard.ais_why:
• On the 2/26/2026 demo, evaluator Michael Henderson praised Zycus's ability to suggest autonomous sourcing beyond the vendor master and asked that conversational tactical sourcing be enabled for mid-size spends (the example was 50 chairs). Mary Stapleton called catalog-in-chat a big step forward for end users. Both are buyer-driven autonomous sourcing signals.
• Collins Farr asked deep orchestration questions, including whether Cornell can upload purchase policies as source documents the AI references when responding to user requests. Michael Henderson asked whether the AI text box could sit in front of SSO and pop up there. These are not check-the-box AI questions; they are deployment-level thinking.
• The need spans Merlin Intake, federated AI search, AI-driven RFQ and negotiation via Anna, AI price benchmarking, and AP Smartdesk. A stakeholder demo is set for mid-March and the path to NDA and budget is defined. The 64-minute demo shows real AI hunger, but until budget is confirmed, confidence sits at Medium.

EB/CFO unmapped and unengaged — Michael Henderson (CPO, champion) explicitly stated on 6 Feb that he and Cooper are 'not the sole decision makers' and must figure out who the internal partners are. A $450k multi-year SaaS deal in higher ed requires CFO or Provost-level sign-off. No EB/CFO meeting is scheduled, no budget-authority conversation logged on any call. Map the CFO/Provost, get Henderson to broker an intro, and put a written ROI case (quantifying the $60M savings goal and the 2026-2028 intercept value) in front of them within 14 days. This is the single highest-leverage move to de-risk the forecast.

If IT pushes back ('why not just wait for Workday eProcurement and sourcing modules in 2028?'), the deal stalls despite CPO enthusiasm. Co-author the IT narrative with Henderson and Cooper NOW: position Zycus as the solution that (a) rationalizes the 140-catalog mess and gives IT a 2-year runway to test 12-15 catalogs for Workday, (b) captures $60M savings in the 2026-2028 window instead of waiting, and (c) persists post-Workday as the best-in-breed intake/sourcing layer, reducing Workday's module footprint and integration tax. Schedule a joint Cornell IT + Zycus + Henderson alignment call within 14 days.

Make the ROI workshop the next milestone — Cornell has quantified pain ($60M savings on $1.1B spend, small team, austerity) but no formal ROI model or CFO-facing business case exists yet. The pricing call 4 Mar likely covered ballpark numbers but not a written proposal. Run an ROI workshop within 14 days with Henderson, Cooper, and ideally the CFO/finance lead: quantify the 2-year intercept-window value (savings captured 2026-2028 vs waiting for Workday Jul 2028), model the Merlin autonomous negotiation ROI (X sourcing events per quarter, Y% savings per event, Z hours saved per event), and build the iSave savings-pipeline dashboard as the CFO reporting layer.
